Assessment Summary




	
		

			Logan is a 29-year-old male with a significant history of opioid (heroin, fentanyl) and stimulant (methamphetamine) use, beginning in adolescence and early adulthood. He reports recent relapse following a period of stability, with last fentanyl use yesterday and heroin use approximately one month ago. Logan is currently maintained on methadone, which has effectively managed withdrawal symptoms. He has a history of depression, anxiety, and ADHD, with current depressive symptoms but no active suicidal ideation or self-harm. He is currently homeless, unemployed, and not engaged in formal treatment or mental health services, though he expresses willingness to participate. His recovery environment is unstable, and he is seeking entry into sober living.

Logan’s ASAM profile indicates mild withdrawal and biomedical risk due to ongoing methadone maintenance and absence of acute medical issues. Emotional and behavioral conditions are moderate, with untreated mental health symptoms and a history of trauma related to the loss of his father. Readiness to change is moderate, as Logan demonstrates insight and willingness but has fluctuating motivation and recent relapse. Relapse risk is high, given recent substance use, history of significant consequences, and current environmental instability. The recovery environment is also high risk due to homelessness and lack of structure.

Relapse risk is elevated by environmental instability, lack of structured supports, and recent substance use. Barriers include homelessness, untreated mental health symptoms, and limited engagement with professional supports. Protective factors include supportive relationships with his mother, family, and girlfriend, as well as prior experience in the recovery field and familiarity with 12-step programs. Logan’s motivation is present but inconsistent, and his insight into the need for change is evident. Environmental concerns, particularly lack of stable housing, remain a significant challenge to sustained recovery.

The recommended level of care is Intensive Outpatient (IOP 2.1), as Logan requires structured clinical support to address high relapse risk, environmental instability, and co-occurring mental health symptoms. Outpatient services alone are insufficient given the severity of relapse and environmental risk, while residential treatment is not indicated due to the absence of acute biomedical or psychiatric instability and Logan’s willingness to engage in less restrictive care. The ASAM profile supports IOP as the least restrictive, medically necessary level to address Logan’s clinical needs and promote recovery stability.

He reports recent relapse following a period of stability, with last fentanyl use yesterday and heroin use approximately one month ago. Logan is currently maintained on methadone, which has effectively managed withdrawal symptoms. He has a history of depression, anxiety, and ADHD, with current depressive symptoms but no active suicidal ideation or self-harm. He is currently homeless, unemployed, and not engaged in formal treatment or mental health services, though he expresses willingness to participate. His recovery environment is unstable, and he is seeking entry into sober living. Logan’s ASAM profile indicates mild withdrawal and biomedical risk due to ongoing methadone maintenance and absence of acute medical issues. Emotional and behavioral conditions are moderate, with untreated mental health symptoms and a history of trauma related to the loss of his father. Readiness to change is moderate, as Logan demonstrates insight and willingness but has fluctuating motivation and recent relapse. Relapse risk is high, given recent substance use, history of significant consequences, and current environmental instability. The recovery environment is also high risk due to homelessness and lack of structure. Relapse risk is elevated by environmental instability, lack of structured supports, and recent substance use. Barriers include homelessness, untreated mental health symptoms, and limited engagement with professional supports. Protective factors include supportive relationships with his mother, family, and girlfriend, as well as prior experience in the recovery field and familiarity with 12-step programs. Logan’s motivation is present but inconsistent, and his insight into the need for change is evident. Environmental concerns, particularly lack of stable housing, remain a significant challenge to sustained recovery. The recommended level of care is Intensive Outpatient (IOP 2.1), as Logan requires structured clinical support to address high relapse risk, environmental instability, and co-occurring mental health symptoms. Outpatient services alone are insufficient given the severity of relapse and environmental risk, while residential treatment is not indicated due to the absence of acute biomedical or psychiatric instability and Logan’s willingness to engage in less restrictive care. The ASAM profile supports IOP as the least restrictive, medically necessary level to address Logan’s clinical needs and promote recovery stability. Goal: Logan will achieve and maintain recovery stability through active participation in structured substance use disorder treatment and ongoing engagement in recovery-oriented services. Objectives: Logan will attend and participate in a minimum of three clinical group or individual sessions per week focused on relapse prevention and recovery skills. Logan will demonstrate consistent engagement with medication-assisted treatment and report any lapses or cravings to his treatment team. Goal: Logan will improve independent living skills and vocational functioning to enhance stability and support sustained recovery. Objectives: Logan will participate in life skills and vocational rehabilitation groups at least once weekly to build skills in employment readiness, financial management, and housing navigation.
